planting.note: Divide clumps in early spring or fall in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or calcareous soil in partial shade; downy alumroot grows naturally in rocky, limestone outcrops, cliff ledges, and calcareous slope forests of the Appalachians and adjacent regions.

directions: Plant in well-drained, slightly alkaline to neutral, rocky or calcareous, humus-rich soil in partial shade; downy alumroot is one of the most rock-garden-friendly native heucheras, naturally growing in the crevices of limestone outcrops and calcareous slope forests of the central Appalachians and surrounding regions. The plant is unusual among heucheras for preferring alkaline to neutral calcareous soils rather than the acidic soils favored by most alumroots. The soft, downy hairs covering the leaves give it an attractive, velvety texture that distinguishes it from harder-textured species. Tall, airy stems of tiny pinkish-white flowers in late spring attract small native bees. In the garden, it performs best in well-drained, calcareous rock garden settings or in partially shaded retaining walls where drainage is excellent.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or gravelly soil in partial shade to full sun; early saxifrage grows naturally on shaded rock outcrops, rocky slope forests, and gravelly woodland edges across eastern North America.

directions: Plant in moist to dry, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, rocky or gravelly, well-drained soil in partial shade; early saxifrage is one of the earliest native wildflowers to bloom in the eastern woodland, producing sprays of tiny, white, star-shaped flowers on slender stems in late winter to early spring while the woodland floor is still cold. The basal rosette of spatula-shaped leaves remains close to the ground all year, providing evergreen ground cover. The plant grows naturally on rocky outcrops, clifftops, and gravelly, shaded slopes where excellent drainage and relatively sparse competition from other vegetation allow it to persist. An excellent early-blooming native for rock gardens and shaded, well-drained slopes, providing critical early nectar for queen bumblebees and early mining bees. The 'saxifrage' name (rock-breaker) references its habit of growing in rock crevices.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, strongly acidic, peaty or gravelly, well-drained to seepage-wet soil in partial shade to full sun; grass-of-parnassus grows in calcareous or acidic seeps, bog edges, and wet limestone glades — moist but never stagnantly flooded.

directions: Plant in moist, peaty or gravelly, slightly acidic to neutral, seepage-wet soil in partial shade to full sun in bog garden or seepage slope settings; grass-of-parnassus is a jewel-like small perennial of cold, wet seeps and bog margins, producing one of the most beautiful and architecturally distinctive flowers of the native flora — a pure white, waxy, five-petaled flower with distinctive green veining and unique, fringe-tipped staminodes (sterile stamens) that are thought to deceive pollinators by mimicking nectaries. The flowers appear in fall, unusually late for such a small, delicate plant. Not a grass at all despite the name, but rather a member of the saxifrage family. A challenging but rewarding native for bog gardens, seepage gardens, and constantly moist, cool, acidic garden settings.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, calcareous to slightly acidic, well-drained to seepage-wet soil in partial shade to full sun; largeleaf parnassia grows in calcareous seeps, limestone glade margins, and wet, rocky, open habitats of the central Appalachians and adjacent regions.

directions: Plant in moist, calcareous (calcium-rich) to slightly acidic, constantly moist but well-drained seepage soil in partial shade to full sun; largeleaf grass-of-parnassus is the larger-leaved and larger-flowered of the eastern Parnassia species, producing elegant pure white flowers with green veining and the decorative fringe-tipped staminodes characteristic of the genus. It grows in calcareous seeps and limestone outcrops in the central Appalachians — habitats with constantly moving, mineral-rich groundwater. The white flowers in fall (August–October) provide late-season nectar at a time when few other wildflowers are blooming in its native habitat. A rare find in native plant nurseries but available from specialist native plant sources. Grows best in calcareous, constantly moist conditions that maintain good drainage without waterlogging.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, well-drained, acidic, rocky or gravelly soil in partial shade; Michaux's saxifrage is an Appalachian high-elevation endemic growing on moist, rocky, north-facing cliffs and rock outcrops above 2000 feet elevation in the southern Blue Ridge Mountains.

directions: Plant in moist, well-drained, acidic, rocky or gravelly, shaded to partially shaded conditions; Michaux's saxifrage is a rare, high-elevation Appalachian endemic named for the French botanist André Michaux who explored the southern Appalachians in the late 18th century. The plant grows on moist, shaded rock outcrops, north-facing cliffs, and rocky streamsides at higher elevations in the Blue Ridge Mountains. Small white flowers appear in summer. The deeply toothed, spatula-shaped leaves with distinctive reddish-brown dried leaf bases are ornamentally attractive in rock garden and alpine trough settings. Only suitable for cool-summer gardens in zones 4–7; does not tolerate heat. A challenging but rewarding plant for the rock garden specialist — available from native plant specialists and botanical garden plant sales.
